final_documents.semantic_identifier: A Developmentally-Staged Transformer Framework for Progressive Cognitive Learning in Language Understanding
final_documents.blurb: Human language understanding develops progressively through stages of cognitive maturation, characterized by increasing memory capacity, attentional control, and abstraction. Inspired by this trajectory, we propose the Developmentally-Staged Transformer for Progressive Cognitive Learning (DST-PCL), a novel framework that models language understanding through staged architectural and functional growth. DST-PCL simulates cognitive development by incrementally expanding Transformer depth, input memory span, and attention complexity across a curriculum-aligned training schedule. Each stage activates a subset of model layers and imposes entropy-regularized attention constraints to reflect bounded human focus. A readiness-based transition controller governs stage advancement based on validation accuracy and attention sharpness. Theoretical analyses guarantee hypothesis class expansion, bounded generalization error, and stability under stage transitions. Empirical evaluations across three benchmark datasets BoolQ, MultiNLI, and ReClor demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines, with up to +5.1% accuracy and +6.3 F1 improvement. Further, attention entropy and interpretability metrics confirm the cognitively plausible progression of model behavior. DST-PCL offers a new paradigm for cognitively grounded NLP systems that unify performance, developmental structure, and interpretability.
match_highlights: Human language understanding develops progressively through stages of cognitive maturation, characterized by increasing memory capacity, attentional control, and abstraction. Inspired by this trajectory, we propose the Developmentally-Staged Transformer for Progressive Cognitive Learning (DST-PCL), a novel framework that models language understanding through staged architectural and functional growth. DST-PCL simulates cognitive development by incrementally expanding Transformer depth, input memory span, and attention complexity across a curriculum-aligned training schedule. Each stage activates a subset of model layers and imposes entropy-regularized attention constraints to reflect bounded human focus. A readiness-based transition controller governs stage advancement based on validation accuracy and attention sharpness. Theoretical analyses guarantee hypothesis class expansion, bounded generalization error, and stability under stage transitions. Empirical evaluations across three benchmark datasets BoolQ, MultiNLI, and ReClor demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines, with up to +5.1% accuracy and +6.3 F1 improvement. Further, attention entropy and interpretability metrics confirm the cognitively plausible progression of model behavior. DST-PCL offers a new paradigm for cognitively grounded NLP systems that unify performance, developmental structure, and interpretability.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: dnnxzz/h-jepa-ssm-moe · Hugging Face
final_documents.blurb:
# H-JEPA-SSM-MoE — hybrid Mamba-2 / local-attention / sparse-MoE backbone (+ char-LM demo checkpoint)
...
An oracle-verified, CPU-trainable reference implementation of a post-Transformer stack — Mamba-2 (SSD) selective state-space layers interleaved 9:1 with sliding-window attention (Samba-style), sparse Mixture-of-Experts FFNs (dropless, noisy top-2, Switch load-balancing), plus a JEPA self-supervised head (EMA target encoder + VICReg).
...
| File | What it is |
| --- | --- |
| `lm_machado.pt`(~6.4 MB) | Demo checkpoint: 1.59M-param character-level LM trained 18 min on a CPU on Memórias Póstumas de Brás Cubas (Machado de Assis, 1881, public domain) |
| `hjepa/`+`train_*.py`+`tests/` | The full implementation (custom code — not`transformers`-compatible) |
| `PAPER.md` | Technical report: architecture, hand-derived adjoint backward, verification methodology, CPU benchmarks |
| `train_log.txt` | Exact training log of the released checkpoint |
...
## Headline numbers (16-core consumer CPU, no GPU)
...
- Mamba-2/SSD trains 7.3× faster than Mamba-1/S6 on CPU (3,550 vs 484 chars/s, identical loss curves, same parameter count). Both implementations included.
- Demo LM: ln(99) = 4.60 → 1.66 val cross-entropy (ppl 5.28/char) in 2,500 steps.
- Generation uses the recurrent O(1)-per-token path (SSM state + windowed KV cache): throughput is flat in sequence length (≈52 chars/s on the reference CPU).
- Every subsystem is tested against an independent oracle (9 tests, max errors 10⁻⁷–10⁻⁵), including the analytic backward of the selective scan vs autograd.
...
```
git clone https://huggingface.co/dnnxzz/h-jepa-ssm-moe
cd h-jepa-ssm-moe
python3 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
.venv/bin/python tests/self_test.py                          # verification suite
mkdir -p out && cp lm_machado.pt out/
.venv/bin/python interactive.py                              # streaming REPL (chat-style)
.venv/bin/python train_text.py sample --prompt "Eu era " --tokens 400
.venv/bin/python train_text.py                               # retrain from scratch (~18 min CPU)
.venv/bin/python train_synthetic.py --preset tiny            # JEPA self-supervised loop

```
...
Toy scale (
...
OTA or downstream claims — see`PAPER.md
...
§5). The corpus
...
public domain; code is MIT

final_documents.semantic_identifier: PediaMedAI/ChildAgentEval
final_documents.blurb:
ChildAgentEval is an interactive evaluation framework for studying whether multimodal
large language model (MLLM) agents can align their reasoning, memory, language, and
error patterns with human developmental stages.
...
The benchmark is inspired by the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC),
but it does not reproduce protected clinical test items. Instead, it translates
psychometrically grounded cognitive constructs into web-based interactive tasks
that agents must complete through browser actions such as clicking, selecting,
typing, and responding under time or memory constraints.
...
ChildAgentEval studies cognitive age alignment: whether an agent can exhibit
age-appropriate behavior across language abstraction, working memory, visual and
fluid reasoning, processing speed, and social explanation style.
...
ChildAgentEval is designed as a model-agnostic evaluation infrastructure rather
than a static public dataset. It administers controlled web tasks, records detailed
interaction traces, and reports both task-level and factor-level results.
...
| --- |
...
Gc | Verbal abstraction, vocabulary, and comprehension
...
| Gf/Gv | Fluid reasoning, visual reasoning, and spatial problem solving
...
| WM | Information
...
The paper evaluates agents across representative developmental anchors: ages 7,
10, 13, and 16. The skill-guided setting uses age bands of 6-8, 9-11, 12-14,
and 15-17.
...
To avoid relying on subjective role prompts such as "act like a child,"
ChildAgentEval introduces a data-grounded skill distillation pipeline. The method
extracts developmental markers from age-stratified child and adolescent corpora,
then distills them into structured cognitive skill cards.

These skill cards constrain the agent through modules for vocabulary abstraction,
working memory, reasoning budget, visual reliance, and social perspective.
...
Standard age prompting does not reliably produce age-ordered behavior. General
purpose agents tend to default to their strongest available capabilities, even
when assigned a younger target age.
...
Skill-guided agents show clearer developmental differentiation in stronger
models. In the reported experiments, targeted cognitive filters produce more
monotonic score trajectories from younger to older age bands, especially in
language-mediated dimensions.
...
Alignment remains uneven across cognitive domains. Language and crystallized
knowledge are easier to calibrate, while working memory, perceptual reasoning,
and processing speed remain harder to align with human developmental norms.
...
![Developmental metrics](assets/figures/developmental-metrics.png)

final_documents.semantic_identifier: JEPA: The Evolution from Perception to World Modeling - JEPA Wiki
final_documents.blurb:
Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) is a self-supervised learning framework proposed by Yann LeCun in his 2022 position paper A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence. As Saining Xie (co-founder of AMI Labs, the company building JEPA into a product) puts it: "JEPA is not a model. JEPA is not a specific algorithm. JEPA is a complete cognitive architecture." It represents a fundamentally different bet on how AI systems should learn about the world — one that breaks from the dominant autoregressive paradigm that powers today's LLMs.
...
JEPA is not an autoregressive model. Unlike
...
, LLa
...
, or any standard LLM, JEPA does not predict the next token in a sequence. It does not operate in discrete
...
generate outputs one element at a
...

...

...

...
4. Next-token prediction doesn't naturally give you a world model. An LLM can predict what text comes next, but it doesn't learn a compact, manipulable model of the world that supports planning. V-JEPA 2 demonstrated that JEPA naturally yields a world model: freeze the encoder, add action conditioning, and you get a robot controller that plans in latent space — something no autoregressive LLM has achieved from self-supervised pretraining alone.
...
Neither paradigm is strictly superior. Autoregressive models excel at language generation and in-context learning. JEPA excels at learning representations, building world models, and planning. The frontier — LLM-JEPA, VL-JEPA, ThinkJEPA— is where the two paradigms meet.
...
- Prediction in latent space: the unifying principle that separates JEPA from autoregressive and generative approaches
...
- Masking strategy drives what you learn: from patches to objects to all-token losses to causal ordering
...
- collapse-prevention is the central challenge: the price of abandoning reconstruction — solved by EMA, SIGReg, deep self-supervision
...
- Modality generality: images
...
video -> audio -> 3D -> point clouds -> language -> vision-language
...

...

...

...
The JEPA family traces an arc that autoregressive models have not: static perception -> dynamic understanding -> world modeling -> causal reasoning -> language-guided planning. Each step adds a capability while preserving the core principle of latent prediction. The open question is whether this arc converges with the autoregressive path — or renders it unnecessary for embodied intelligence.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: BENCHMARKS.md
final_documents.blurb:
**Honesty rules applied:** no tuning on the test set, no hand-picked seeds, honest-scale (multi-seed) where applicable. The only head-to-head comparison is LongMemEval vs mempalace; every other number is our own metric (no competitor column). Where a number missed its target or regressed, it says so plainly.
...
## Our own metrics (no competitor column)
...
| **Rescue@10** (post-contradiction current fact) | **1.000** | After a fact is updated/contradicted, the *current* winning fact still ranks top-10 — where flat-vector stores collapse because the stale fact is often the more similar one. Honest-scale: 3 seeds × 1000 sessions × 2 slices. |
...
| **Personal-fact drift** (recall@10) | **0.9933** | retention_loss@10 = 0.0067. Honest-scale: 3 seeds × (50 facts, 50 sessions, 30 intervening). |
...
| **Sleep-consolidation** (recall@10 preserved) | **1.000 → 1.000** (Δ=0) | Recall survives a full consolidation cycle. 3 seeds × 160-record corpus (20 targets + 40 confusors + 100 noise). |
...
| **MOSAIC community-detection parity** | **36/36** LFR-gauntlet + 10/10 | NMI vs ground-truth on karate / football / LFR n=1000 & 5000; 5× replay-deterministic; modularity-monotonic. |
...
| **Recall p95 latency** | 77 ms @100 · 105 ms @1k · **368 ms @10k** | Misses the internal <100 ms@10k target at scale; the rank/centrality stage dominates. |
...
| **Memory footprint (RSS)** | **589 MB @10,000 records** | Threshold 2,000 MB; passed. Embedder + graph runtime. |
...
- **Recall p95 at 10k = 368 ms** — above the internal <100 ms target. The rank/centrality stage dominates (betweenness recompute is ~1.7 s@10k, mitigated by a centrality cache that stays on by default). A latency-optimization candidate.
...
- **Historical-verbatim retrieval regressed 0.90 → 0.71 in this release** — the ability to retrieve the *superseded/archived* wording of an updated fact verbatim dropped. This is **separate from Rescue@10** (current-fact retrieval, unchanged at 1.000). Likely cause (per an external code review): the centrality-engine swap dropped Hebbian edge-weights (unweighted Brandes), shifting the seed-score landscape toward older densely-connected facts. A tracked fix for the next release.
...
**"Best-benchmarked" holds as breadth + reproducibility + honesty**, plus genuine leads on session-start budget, personal-fact retention, post-contradiction rescue, local-first operation, and deterministic community-detection parity. It does **not** hold as "tops the LongMemEval raw leaderboard" — there it is a 2-question near-tie. The honest posture: **on par with the best on LongMemEval raw, and decisively ahead on longitudinal recall.**

| **Recall p95 latency** | 77 ms @100 · 105 ms @1k · **368 ms @10k** | Misses the internal <100 ms@10k target at scale; the rank/centrality stage dominates. |
...
| **Memory footprint (RSS)** | **589 MB @10,000 records** | Threshold 2,000 MB; passed. Embedder + graph runtime. |
...
- **Recall p95 at 10k = 368 ms** — above the internal <100 ms target. The rank/centrality stage dominates (betweenness recompute is ~1.7 s@10k, mitigated by a centrality cache that stays on by default). A latency-optimization candidate.
...
- **Historical-verbatim retrieval regressed 0.90 → 0.71 in this release** — the ability to retrieve the *superseded/archived* wording of an updated fact verbatim dropped. This is **separate from Rescue@10** (current-fact retrieval, unchanged at 1.000). Likely cause (per an external code review): the centrality-engine swap dropped Hebbian edge-weights (unweighted Brandes), shifting the seed-score landscape toward older densely-connected facts. A tracked fix for the next release.
...
**"Best-benchmarked" holds as breadth + reproducibility + honesty**, plus genuine leads on session-start budget, personal-fact retention, post-contradiction rescue, local-first operation, and deterministic community-detection parity. It does **not** hold as "tops the LongMemEval raw leaderboard" — there it is a 2-question near-tie. The honest posture: **on par with the best on LongMemEval raw, and decisively ahead on longitudinal recall.**

final_documents.semantic_identifier: Introducing the V-JEPA 2 world model and new benchmarks for physical reasoning
final_documents.blurb:
V-JEPA 2 is a 1.2 billion-parameter model that was built using Meta Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture(JEPA), which we first shared in 2022. Our previous work has shown that JEPA performs well for modalities like images and 3D point clouds. Building on V-JEPA, our first model trained on video that we released last year, V-JEPA 2 improves action prediction and world modeling capabilities that enable robots to interact with unfamiliar objects and environments to complete a task. We’re also sharing three new benchmarks to help the research community evaluate how well their existing models learn and reason about the world using video. By sharing this work, we aim to give researchers and developers access to the best models and benchmarks to help accelerate research and progress—ult
...
AI systems that will help enhance people’s
...
As we continue to make advancements in the field of world models, we’re excited to share our work and support progress in the open source community. We’re releasing three new benchmarks to evaluate how well existing models can understand and reason about the physical world from video. While humans perform well on all three benchmarks (85% – 95% accuracy), there’s a notable gap between human performance and that of top models including V-JEPA 2, indicating important directions for models to improve in.

CausalVQA measures the ability of video-language models to answer questions related to physical cause-and-effect. The benchmark is designed to focus on causal understanding in physical-world videos, including questions about counterfactuals (what would have happened if...), anticipation (what might happen next), and planning (what action should occur next to accomplish a goal). We find that while large multimodal models are increasingly capable of answering questions about “what happened” in the video, they still struggle to answer questions about “what could have happened” and “what might happen next,” revealing a substantial gap with respect to human performance on predicting how the physical world will likely evolve given the space of actions and events.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: The ARC of Progress towards AGI: A Living Survey of Abstraction and Reasoning
final_documents.blurb:
6, with updates at https://nimi-ai.com/arc-survey
...
I-1
...
training and toward
...
breakthrough aligns with Chollet
...
ARC-AGI as a computational probe of generalization. ARC-AGI represents a further step in this evolution. Rather than primarily asking how well a system performs relative to others, ARC-AGI is best read as a probe of how a system generalizes from minimal evidence. In this sense, the benchmark asks not "how smart is this system?" but "what kind of intelligence does this system possess?" This framing matters because the same score can be achieved by qualitatively different routes. A system that reaches high accuracy by exploiting benchmark-specific regularities or memorizing training patterns exhibits a different competence profile than a system that extracts reusable primitives and composes them to solve novel tasks. To answer RQ1, we argue that ARC-AGI scores should be interpreted as evidence of understanding only insofar as they are accompanied by mechanism-relevant context. Concretely, performance metrics become meaningful when reported together with the following axes presented in Table 7 .
...
What changes at test time, and how? Does
...
The Compositional Generalization Bottleneck. Compositional generalization, the ability to systematically recombine known primitives in novel ways, represents the binding constraint on current approaches. Systems achieving 70-80% on ARC-AGI-1 consistently drop to 20-30% on ARC-AGI-2 and below 15% on ARC-AGI-3 [Chollet et al. 2025a,b] . This is not gradual degradation but catastrophic failure once compositional depth exceeds system capacity. The challenge manifests through multiple failure modes. First, combinatorial explosion in search spaces makes deeper compositions intractable. ConceptSearch generates 400 candidates for two-step transformations but 8,000 for three-step transformations [Singhal and Shroff 2025] . Without mechanisms to prune search spaces through principled abstraction, the exponential growth of hypothesis spaces overwhelms computational budgets. Second, insufficient learning signal plagues gradient-based approaches. MindsAI achieves strong ARC-AGI-1 results but admits that three-step reasoning provides inadequate training signal for their learning mechanisms. The distribution shift from ARC-AGI-1 to ARC-AGI-2 is not merely quantitative but qualitative, requiring generalization along compositional dimensions absent from training data.
...

...

final_documents.semantic_identifier: Augmenting Von Neumann’s Architecture for an Intelligent Future
final_documents.blurb:
Evaluating the capabilities of a hybrid cognitive computing system requires a new class of benchmarks that move beyond traditional metrics such as FLOPS, cache hits, or raw instruction throughput. The Reasoning Unit (RU), as a symbolic and cognitive co-processor, introduces qualitatively different execution semantics that necessitate metrics sensitive to logic inference, planning complexity, symbolic expressivity, and multi-agent reasoning dynamics. Benchmarks must be constructed not merely to measure how fast instructions are processed, but to quantify how effectively the architecture performs knowledge-driven tasks, manages uncertainty, adapts to goals, and coordinates distributed agents under logical constraints.
...
One essential benchmark category focuses on Symbolic Inference Latency, which measures the time-to-conclusion for various inference paradigms such as backward chaining, forward chaining, and Horn clause resolution. This metric highlights the RU’s ability to traverse rule spaces and belief networks efficiently, revealing both architectural throughput and rule application effectiveness. Another key area of measurement is Agent Coordination Efficiency, which captures the temporal and resource complexity of resolving shared or conflicting goals among multiple concurrent agents. This includes metrics like total decision latency, context-switch overhead between agent threads, and plan convergence depth in collaborative environments.
...
Additionally, Semantic Planning Accuracy quantifies how successfully the RU synthesizes valid, context-sensitive action sequences within partially observable domains, often expressed through logical frameworks like STRIPS or PDDL. Benchmark tasks here evaluate not only the completeness of planning but the RU’s adaptability to ambiguous or under-specified goal formulations. In hybrid architectures, LLM Invocation Overhead becomes another crucial metric. It measures the round-trip latency, schema-conformance rate, and utility of neural responses when symbolic agents delegate subgoals to large language models or other sub-symbolic systems. Finally, Energy per Cognitive Task offers an energy-aware performance indicator, estimating the joules consumed per successful inference cycle or goal resolution. This metric is critical for deploying cognitive computing in constrained or embedded systems, where symbolic efficiency must be maintained under power limitations.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: Measuring Progress Toward AGI: A Cognitive Framework
final_documents.blurb:
Despite widespread discussion of AGI, there is no clear framework for measuring progress toward it. This ambiguity fuels subjective claims, makes it difficult to track progress, and risks hindering responsible governance. As a starting point to address this gap, we present a framework for understanding system capabilities in relation to human cognitive abilities. Drawing from decades of research in psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science, we introduce a Cognitive Taxonomy that deconstructs general intelligence into 10 key cognitive faculties. We then propose a rigorous evaluation protocol in which a system’s performance is measured across a suite of targeted, held-out cognitive tasks, generating a ‘cognitive profile’ that can be used to understand a system’s strengths and weaknesses. We hope this framework will provide a practical roadmap and an initial step toward more rigorous, empirical evaluation of AGI.
...
To understand the cognitive capabilities of an AI system, we need to robustly evaluate the system across each cognitive faculty and compare the system to a meaningful human baseline. We therefore propose the following three-stage evaluation protocol:
...
cognitive profiles to map
...
The first step in evaluating progress toward AGI is to evaluate system performance on a broad suite of cognitive tasks covering each faculty. The cognitive tasks should be:
...
Using the evaluation results from the cognitive assessment and the human baselines, we can next build cognitive profiles for each system to map out the strengths and weaknesses of the system relative to human performance across the 10 cognitive faculties.
...
Here we provide a framework for measuring AI systems across the range of human cognitive capabilities. To execute on this framework, we need robust cognitive benchmarks. Many useful benchmarks already exist for some parts of the cognitive space, including problem solving [Phan et al., 2025], perception [Patraucean et al., 2023], and world knowledge [Cheng et al., 2025, Haas et al., 2025]. But there are large coverage gaps in areas such as metacognition, attention, learning, and social cognition. Moreover, many of the high-quality benchmarks that do exist are fully public, so they are susceptible to data contamination and may not provide generalizable signal. For this reason, we do not think existing benchmarks are sufficient to reliably evaluate AI cognitive capabilities.

ThinkJEPA is a dual-path embodied prediction framework in which a vision-language model acts as a cortex-like reasoner for high-level semantics and long-horizon intent, while a JEPA branch acts as a cerebellum-like controller for low-level dynamics, physical consistency, and rapid local correction. This repository is the public ThinkJEPA release for reproducing the released training and evaluation setup on EgoDex-style data and cache inputs.

 
 
 
 
 
 

## Overview
...
- The VLM-thinker branch provides high-level reasoning guidance from Qwen3-VL-Thinking features.
- The dense JEPA branch models video dynamics and supplies low-level embodied features for prediction.
- The released training path predicts future trajectory outputs from JEPA features conditioned by pyramid guidance from the VLM branch.
- This public snapshot is intentionally minimal: it includes the core train/eval code, preprocessing scripts, retained EgoDex helpers, and a bundled `vjepa2/` dependency subtree required by the released path.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: CogDevelop2K: Reversed Cognitive Development in Multimodal Large Language Models
final_documents.blurb:
Are Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) stochastic parrots? Do they genuinely understand? This paper aims to explore the core cognitive abilities that human intelligence builds upon to perceive, comprehend, and reason in MLLMs. To this end, we propose CogDevelop2K, a comprehensive benchmark that spans 12 sub-concepts from primitive knowledge like object permanence and boundary to more complex abilities like intentionality understanding, structured via the developmental trajectory of a human mind. We evaluate 46 MLLMs on our benchmarks. Surprisingly, we observe a reversed cognitive developmental trajectory compared to humans. Comprehensively, we further evaluate the influence of evaluation strategies and prompting techniques.
...
As highlighted in previous
...
To evaluate the performance of MLLMs on the core cognitive abilities, we curate the first-ever vision cognitive development benchmark, termed as CogDeveop2K, which consists of a total of 2519 questions with 2517 images and 455 videos. Then, we evaluate 46 MLLM models on our benchmark that spans all four stages of cognitive development. We introduce a novel multi-frame question format to evaluate models’ co-reference, cognitive reasoning and temporal understanding capability simultaneously. Forty-seven models are compared against a human baseline under zero-shot conditions using 11 different prompts (including no prompt). Surprisingly, while prompts can boost model performance by 8.1%, models still demonstrate reversed trends in cognitive development against those observed in children.
...
We systematically evaluate 48 Multi-modal Large Language Models on the CogDevelop2K benchmark, which spans 12 cognitive concepts designed to assess a broad range of the developmental trajectory of Multi-modal Large Language Models. These abilities substantiate core cognition ranging from object permanence and boundary to mechanical reasoning and intentionality understanding. The models were tested across multiple question formats and ten prompt variations, yielding a comprehensive assessment of their core cognition. For example, in the sensorimotor stage, GPT families show moderate performance, with accuracy scores between 0.4 and 0.6. In the concrete operational stage, GPT families show lower performance, with accuracy scores between 0.2 and 0.4. Nevertheless, in the formal operational stage, GPT families show stronger performance, with accuracy scores between 0.6 and 0.8. Surprisingly, we find an inverse cognitive developmental trajectory compared to humans in more advanced models, which are typically regarded as state-of-the-art (Fig. 3 and Fig. 4).
...
, a novel

final_documents.semantic_identifier: PabloCabaleiro/pondera
final_documents.blurb:
Pondera is a lightweight, YAML-first framework to evaluate AI models and agents with pluggable runners and an LLM-as-a-judge.
...
Lightweight, YAML‑first evaluation for AI models and agents. You write cases in YAML, provide a tiny async runner that returns markdown, and get a strict rubric‑scored JSON judgment back. No framework lock‑in.
...
- Single source of truth (YAML).
- Any model/provider (you control inference).
- Strict JSON judgment schema with weighted rubric scoring.
- Simple Python API (CLI removed).
- Reproducible artifacts (answer, judgment, summary, meta). Per‑case rubric overrides.
...
Defines one evaluation: query, optional attachments, expectations, thresholds, optional per‑case rubric.
...
- **Runner (you)**: produces an answer markdown given the case input. Must implement:
...
- **Judge (built-in default)**: the bundled class `Judge` (import with `from pondera.judge import Judge`) scores with a rubric and returns a strict `Judgment`. It is the default expectation. You can override it by passing any object matching `JudgeProtocol` (async `judge(...) -> Judgment`). Custom example:
...
`evaluate_case_async` is the real coroutine that performs the evaluation (single or multi‑repetition). Use it inside async code (`await evaluate_case_async(...)`).
...
`evaluate_case` is a thin convenience wrapper for synchronous contexts: it calls `asyncio.run` on `evaluate_case_async` and raises if an event loop is already running (to prevent nested loop issues).
...
Return type is always `MultiEvaluationResult` for a stable downstream API. When repetitions == 1 the object contains exactly one `EvaluationResult` in `evaluations[0]` and aggregates are computed over that single sample.
...
1. Install: `uv add pondera`
2. Write a case YAML under `eval/cases/`
3. Implement a runner with `async run(...)-> {"answer": str}`
4. Call `evaluate_case(path, runner=RunnerImpl(), judge=Judge())`
5. Use `multi.evaluations[0]` for single runs or iterate for reproducibility studies
...
**Per case directory** (if you persist):
...
- `answer.md` (model's answer)
- `judgment.json` (judgment schema)
- `judge_prompt.txt` (raw prompt sent to the judge, including any inlined file snippets; only created when non‑empty)
- `meta.json` (thresholds, timings, pass flag, runner metadata)
- `summary.md` (human readable summary)
...
**Multi evaluation**:
...
- Aggregated stats (min / max / mean / median / stdev / variance) per criterion + overall inside `multi/aggregates.json` with a human summary.

final_documents.semantic_identifier: Value-guided action planning with JEPA world models
final_documents.blurb:
# Value-guided action planning with JEPA world models
...
Building deep learning models that can reason about their environment requires capturing its underlying dynamics. Joint-Embedded Predictive Architectures (JEPA) provide a promising framework to model such dynamics by learning representations and predictors through a self-supervised prediction objective. However, their ability to support effective action planning remains limited. We propose an approach to enhance planning with JEPA world models by shaping their representation space so that the negative goal-conditioned value function for a reaching cost in a given environment is approximated by a distance (or quasi-distance) between state embeddings. We introduce a practical method to enforce this constraint during training and show that it leads to significantly improved planning performance compared to standard JEPA models on simple control tasks.
...
AI-based Problem Solving and Planning, Reinforcement Learning in Robotics, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), Computer science, Constraint (computer-aided design)

final_documents.blurb:
We perform an ensemble analysis, combining systems to achieve better results than any system alone and analysing individual strengths
...
, it is sobering to see
...
strategies they employ to solve ARC could
...
Finally, we publish the arckit Python library
...
To systematically build and evaluate computer systems that can solve abstract reasoning problems in a human-like intelligent way, we turn to a concrete benchmark. In 2019, the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) was introduced, as an attempt to codify a benchmark of intelligence 2- a sort of ‘IQ Test’ for AI. The ARC contains a series of human-designed tasks on grids, which require learning some transformation from a small number of demonstrations. Despite three international competitions with over $100,000 in prize money, performance on ARC has proved elusive, with no single system surpassing 50% accuracy on the ARC-Easy dataset or 20% on the test set 5.
...
With only a handful of training examples per ARC task, and \(10^{900}\) possible answers (of which exactly one gains credit), traditional machine learning (ML) methods that require large datasets have so far been unable to make progress. Current state-of-the-art approaches to ARC make use of complex hand-crafted algorithms based on brute force search, without harnessing any ML 6. This work looks at whether novel machine learning systems that focus on abstraction and reasoning can achieve broad generalisation, using ARC as a focal point.
...
We adapt the DreamCoder algorithm, a recent state-of-the-art algorithm for program induction, to solve ARC tasks. DreamCoder writes programs in a domain-specific language to solve tasks; we design the Perceptual Abstraction & Reasoning Language (PeARL) pure functional domain specific language for this purpose. Our DreamCoder implementation solves \(3\times\) more tasks than existing work 7. We introduce a new framework for solving ARC tasks using large-language models (LLMs), transforming these visual tasks into a textual domain. A detailed evaluation of three model classes8,9, 10 shows that LLMs can achieve competitive performance with human-crafted systems with the right augmentation and domain transformation. We build an ensemble of multiple ARC solvers that outperforms any individual system, and analyse the diversity and relative strengths of each solver.
...
The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC), introduced by Chollet in 2, attempts to provide a benchmark for broad generalisation. By formalising a concrete way to measure generalisation ability, the hope is to foster progress in much the same way as ImageNet transformed image classification.

obj.content:
# Benchmarking the Ponder Engine: A Cognitive Architecture Evaluation Framework

You're right — this isn't a RAG, chatbot, vector DB, or LLM. It's a **developmental cognitive architecture** with a JEPA-gated SSM core, multiple cognitive modules, reconsolidation, prospective memory, and staged maturation. That means standard ML benchmarks (perplexity, MMLU, retrieval precision) are largely **category errors** when applied to it. You need a fundamentally different evaluation philosophy.

---

## The Core Problem

Cognitive architectures are evaluated differently from narrow AI systems because they're **integrated collections of capabilities** rather than single-purpose models [[15]](https://cdn.aaai.org/Workshops/2007/WS-07-04/WS07-04-015.pdf). As the CAIE framework argues, what matters is not just *what* the system can do but *how* it does it — the mechanisms, the developmental trajectory, and the generalization profile [[42]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-026-11493-x).

For your specific architecture, I'd organize benchmarking into **five dimensions**, each targeting a different aspect of what makes the ponder engine unique:

---

## Dimension 1: Developmental Trajectory

Your architecture has explicit developmental stages. This is one of its most distinctive features and should be benchmarked directly.

**What to measure:** Does the system actually progress through stages in a meaningful way? Does each stage unlock qualitatively new capabilities?

**Approach:** Use **CogDevelop2K** [[35]](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.10855v2) or **VECA** (Bayley-4-based) [[46]](https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v36i1.19877) as inspiration. These test cognitive capabilities across developmental stages — object permanence, boundary detection, mechanical reasoning, intentionality understanding. You'd run the same battery at each developmental stage and verify:

- **Stage-appropriate performance:** Earlier stages should succeed on simpler tasks and fail on complex ones
- **Monotonic progression:** Later stages should strictly dominate earlier ones (no regression)
- **Qualitative shifts:** Not just "better at the same thing" but genuinely new capabilities emerging

**Custom metric:** *Developmental Gain* — the ratio of capability breadth at stage N+1 vs stage N, measured across a held-out task suite.

---

## Dimension 2: World Model Quality (JEPA-Specific)

Your core primitive is a JEPA-gated SSM. The JEPA component learns a world model by predicting in latent space. This needs its own evaluation independent of downstream task performance.

**What to measure:** How good is the internal world model? This is the "engine" part of the ponder engine.

**Approach:** Borrow from the JEPA-WM evaluation framework [[40]](https://arxiv.org/html/2512.24497v2)[[52]](https://github.com/facebookresearch/jepa-wms):

| Metric | What it captures |
|---|---|
| **Latent prediction error** (MSE over rollout) | How accurately the predictor forecasts future latent states |
| **Rollout stability** | Does error compound or stay bounded over long horizons? |
| **Proprioceptive decoding error** | Can you decode the agent's own state from the latent? |
| **Visual decoding quality** (LPIPS) | Can you reconstruct future observations from predicted latents? |
| **Planning success rate** | Given a goal embedding, can the model plan action sequences? |

**Key insight from the JEPA-WM literature:** These metrics should be tracked *independently* of planning performance because they measure model quality rather than optimizer quality [[40]](https://arxiv.org/html/2512.24497v2). A good world model with a bad planner is a different problem from a bad world model with a good planner.

---

## Dimension 3: Memory & Consolidation Dynamics

Your architecture has reconsolidation counting, prospective memory, consolidation experts, and MoE SSM replay buffers. This is a sophisticated memory subsystem that needs its own evaluation.

**What to measure:** Does the memory system actually consolidate, forget, and reconsolidate in useful ways?

**Approach:** Adapt from the biologically-grounded memory evaluation literature [[39]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.08538)[[23]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.08538)[[24]](https://www.tdcommons.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi):

| Metric | What to test |
|---|---|
| **Retention precision** | After consolidation, what fraction of stored facts are still retrievable? |
| **Store reduction** | How much does consolidation compress the memory store? |
| **Reconsolidation accuracy** | When a memory is retrieved and updated, does the new version correctly supersede the old? |
| **Forgetting curve shape** | Does it follow a principled (Ebbinghaus-like) decay, or is it random? |
| **Sleep consolidation gain** | Does offline consolidation improve retrieval vs. no-consolidation baseline? |
| **Prospective memory hit rate** | Can the system remember to perform intended actions at the right future moment? |

**Custom benchmark:** Create a *Contradiction Stream* — feed the system a sequence of facts where some are later contradicted, then test whether it retrieves the *current* fact (not the stale one). This directly tests reconsolidation. The "Rescue@10" metric from the IAI-MCP benchmarks [[51]](https://github.com/CodeAbra/iai-mcp/blob/main/BENCHMARKS.md) is a good template: after contradiction, does the current fact still rank in the top-10 retrievals?

---

## Dimension 4: Fluid Intelligence & Generalization

This is where ARC-AGI comes in. Your architecture isn't an LLM, but it has a reasoning core. ARC tests the ability to solve *novel* problems from very few examples — exactly the kind of generalization a cognitive architecture should demonstrate [[41]](https://arcprize.org/arc-agi)[[45]](https://github.com/fchollet/ARC/blob/master/README.md)[[53]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.13372).

**What to measure:** Can the system reason from sparse demonstrations on tasks it has never seen?

**Approach:**

- **ARC-AGI-1** (400 evaluation tasks): The established benchmark. Human baseline ~80%, current AI SOTA ~50-55% on the public set [[57]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-73582-7).
- **ARC-AGI-2**: Harder, tests deeper compositional generalization. Systems at 70-80% on ARC-1 drop to 20-30% here [[53]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.13372).
- **ARC-AGI-3**: Interactive reasoning — the system must actively explore and learn [[49]](https://arcprize.org/arc-agi/3).

**Why this matters for your architecture:** ARC tests *fluid intelligence* (novel problem-solving) rather than *crystallized intelligence* (memorized knowledge). Your JEPA-gated SSM with developmental stages should, in principle, be capable of extracting reusable primitives and composing them — exactly what ARC measures. The gap between ARC-1 and ARC-2 performance is particularly diagnostic of whether the system is genuinely composing primitives or pattern-matching.

---

## Dimension 5: Cognitive Control & Adaptive Computation

This is the "ponder" part — the architecture should allocate more computation to harder problems.

**What to measure:** Does the system adapt its computational depth to problem difficulty?

**Approach:** Inspired by PonderNet [[6]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2107.05407) and CogPonder [[3]](https://openreview.net/pdf):

| Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Pondering steps vs. difficulty correlation** | Do harder inputs trigger more SSM recurrence steps? |
| **Compute-accuracy frontier** | At each compute budget, what accuracy is achieved? Is the frontier Pareto-optimal? |
| **Overthinking penalty** | On simple inputs, does the system waste computation? |
| **Underthinking penalty** | On complex inputs, does it stop too early? |
| **Halting calibration** | Is the halting probability well-calibrated to actual task difficulty? |

**Custom test:** Create a *difficulty ladder* — a set of tasks with known complexity gradations (e.g., parity of N bits for N=2 through N=20, or multi-step reasoning with 1-10 hops). Plot pondering steps against N. A working ponder mechanism should show a clear positive correlation.

---

## Dimension 6 (Bonus): Disturbance Detection & Anomaly Sensitivity

Your architecture has a dedicated Disturbance Detector module. This is unusual and worth benchmarking directly.

**What to measure:** Can the system detect when its world model is violated?

**Approach:** Use the *subspace-decomposed JEPA* methodology [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.31111v1) as inspiration — track the angular velocity of the progression subspace. When the world model encounters something unexpected, the latent should "spike." Test with:

- **Controlled perturbations:** Introduce physically impossible events in a simulation and measure detection latency
- **Gradual distribution shift:** How early does the disturbance detector fire?
- **False positive rate:** Does it cry wolf on benign novelty?

---

## Putting It All Together: A Benchmarking Protocol

I'd recommend a **staged evaluation protocol** that mirrors your architecture's own developmental stages:

### Phase 1: Component-Level (Unit Tests)
- JEPA latent prediction error on synthetic sequences
- SSM recurrence throughput and state fidelity
- Gate activation patterns on controlled inputs
- Bonsai verification accuracy against Oracle (DeepSeek)

### Phase 2: Module-Level (Integration Tests)
- Disturbance Detector: perturbation detection AUROC
- Consolidation: retention precision, store reduction, forgetting curves
- Prospective memory: intention completion rate
- Self-Model: proprioceptive decoding error

### Phase 3: System-Level (Cognitive Battery)
- **CogDevelop2K-style** developmental stage verification
- **ARC-AGI-1** for fluid intelligence baseline
- **Difficulty-ladder** for adaptive computation calibration
- **Contradiction stream** for reconsolidation testing

### Phase 4: Longitudinal (Developmental)
- Run the full battery at each developmental stage
- Track *Developmental Gain* across stages
- Verify no catastrophic forgetting between stages
- Measure the compute-accuracy frontier at each stage

---

## What NOT to Do

- **Don't benchmark against LLM perplexity or MMLU** — your architecture doesn't do next-token prediction and wasn't trained on web text. It's measuring the wrong thing.
- **Don't use RAG retrieval metrics** (NDCG, MRR) — unless you're specifically testing the memory retrieval subsystem in isolation.
- **Don't treat any single number as "the score"** — cognitive architectures need *profiles*, not leaderboard positions. The CAIE framework [[42]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-026-11493-x) and the Cognitive Taxonomy approach [[54]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.28405v1) both emphasize multi-dimensional cognitive profiles over single aggregate scores.

---

## A Concrete Starting Point

If I had to pick the **minimum viable benchmark suite** to start with:

1. **ARC-AGI-1** (public 400-task eval set) — establishes fluid reasoning baseline
2. **A custom contradiction stream** (100 facts, 30 contradictions, measure Rescue@10) — tests reconsolidation
3. **A difficulty ladder** (parity or multi-hop QA, 10 difficulty levels) — tests adaptive pondering
4. **JEPA rollout MSE** on a simple physics environment — tests world model quality
5. **Stage-transition verification** — run (1) and (3) at each developmental stage

This gives you coverage across all five dimensions with relatively low implementation overhead. The ARC and difficulty ladder are off-the-shelf; the contradiction stream and JEPA rollout metrics require custom setup but are well-defined.

---

Would you like me to dive deeper into any of these dimensions, or help design the specific contradiction stream / difficulty ladder protocols for your architecture?

obj.reasoning: The user wants me to look at a specific URL - the README for EnterpriseRAG-Bench from the onyx-dot-app GitHub repository. Let me search for information about this benchmark.

The user is asking me to look at the EnterpriseRAG-Bench README and consider how it might relate to benchmarking their "ponder engine" cognitive architecture. Let me analyze what I found and give a thoughtful response about whether/how this benchmark is relevant.

From the search results, EnterpriseRAG-Bench is:
- A RAG benchmark by Onyx for company internal documents
- ~500,000 documents across 9 enterprise source types (Slack, Gmail, Linear, Google Drive, HubSpot, Fireflies, GitHub, Jira, Confluence)
- 500 questions across 10 categories
- Categories include: Basic, Semantic, Intra-Document Reasoning, Project Related, Constrained, Conflicting Info, Completeness, Miscellaneous, High-level, Info Not Found
- Metrics: Correctness, Completeness, Document Recall, Invalid Extra Documents
- The corpus has cross-document coherence, realistic noise (misfiled docs, near-duplicates, conflicting info)

The user's question is essentially: "What about this benchmark for my ponder engine?" Given that the ponder engine is NOT a RAG system, this is an interesting question. Let me think about what's relevant here.

The key insight is that EnterpriseRAG-Bench tests several capabilities that ARE relevant to the ponder engine's cognitive modules, even though the overall framing is RAG:

1. **Conflicting Info category** (20 questions) - documents that directly contradict each other. This directly tests reconsolidation and the disturbance detector.
2. **Intra-Document Reasoning** (50 questions) - combining info from distant sections of a single document. Tests the SSM's ability to maintain state over long context.
3. **Completeness** (10 questions) - requires fetching ALL relevant documents. Tests memory consolidation and retrieval completeness.
4. **Constrained** (30 questions) - multiple relevant docs with qualifiers that eliminate all but one answer. Tests the gate mechanism and selective attention.
5. **Project Related** (40 questions) - aggregate knowledge from related documents. Tests associative memory and cross-referencing.
6. **Info Not Found** - recognizing when information is absent. Tests the disturbance detector / anomaly sensitivity.
7. **High-level** - answers not in any single document. Tests the JEPA world model's ability to synthesize/abstract.

So while EnterpriseRAG-Bench is framed as a RAG benchmark, its question categories actually probe cognitive capabilities that map surprisingly well onto the ponder engine's modules. The question is whether to use it as-is or adapt it.

Let me give a thoughtful, nuanced answer about this.

final_documents.blurb:
# EnterpriseRAG-Bench: A RAG Benchmark for Company Internal Knowledge
...
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the standard approach for grounding large language models in information that was not available during training. While existing datasets and benchmarks focus on web or other public sources, there is still no widely adopted dataset that realistically reflects the nature of company-internal knowledge. Meanwhile, startups, enterprises, and researchers are increasingly developing AI Agents designed to operate over exactly this kind of proprietary data. To close this gap, we release a synthetic enterprise corpus, its generation framework, and a leaderboard. We present EnterpriseRAG-Bench, a dataset consisting of approximately 500,000 documents spanning nine enterprise source types (Slack, Gmail, Linear, Google Drive, HubSpot, Fireflies, GitHub, Jira, and Confluence) and 500 questions across ten categories that test distinct retrieval and reasoning capabilities. The corpus is generated with cross-document coherence (grounded in shared projects, people, and initiatives) and augmented with realistic noise such as misfiled documents, near-duplicates, and conflicting information. The question set ranges from simple single-document lookups to multi-document reasoning, constrained retrieval, conflict resolution, and recognizing when information is absent. The generation framework lets teams generate variants tailored to their own industry, scale, and source mix. The dataset, code, evaluation harness, and leaderboard are available at https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench.

# EnterpriseRAG-Bench: A RAG Benchmark for Company Internal Knowledge
...
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the standard approach for grounding large language models in information that was not available during training. While existing datasets and benchmarks focus on web or other public sources, there is still no widely adopted dataset that realistically reflects the nature of company-internal knowledge. Meanwhile, startups, enterprises, and researchers are increasingly developing AI Agents designed to operate over exactly this kind of proprietary data. To close this gap, we release a synthetic enterprise corpus, its generation framework, and a leaderboard. We present EnterpriseRAG-Bench, a dataset consisting of approximately 500,000 documents spanning nine enterprise source types (Slack, Gmail, Linear, Google Drive, HubSpot, Fireflies, GitHub, Jira, and Confluence) and 500 questions across ten categories that test distinct retrieval and reasoning capabilities. The corpus is generated with cross-document coherence (grounded in shared projects, people, and initiatives) and augmented with realistic noise such as misfiled documents, near-duplicates, and conflicting information. The question set ranges from simple single-document lookups to multi-document reasoning, constrained retrieval, conflict resolution, and recognizing when information is absent. The generation framework lets teams generate variants tailored to their own industry, scale, and source mix. The dataset, code, evaluation harness, and leaderboard are available at https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench.

# IndustryRAG-Bench
...
## 📖 Overview
...
IndustryRAG Bench provides a dataset for company internal documents as well as a way of generating similar datasets for different industries, scales of companies, etc. Existing Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) and Information Retrieval (IR) datasets largely focus on publically accessible document sets (Bing searches, Stack Overflow forums, etc.). Up until now, there hasn't been a publically accessible dataset which focuses entirely on company internal data. This dataset/repo aims to provide that.
...
The dataset consists of ~450,000 documents and 500 questions. It simulates a company called "Redwood Inference" that provides AI model inference as a service.
...
00 docs

obj.content:
# EnterpriseRAG-Bench and the Ponder Engine

Great find. This is worth a careful look because — while it's framed as a RAG benchmark — several of its **question categories probe cognitive capabilities that map surprisingly well onto your architecture's modules**. It's not a perfect fit, but it's more relevant than you might expect, and more importantly, it gives you a **realistic enterprise corpus** to stress-test against rather than a toy dataset.

---

## What EnterpriseRAG-Bench Actually Is

It's a benchmark built by Onyx consisting of ~500,000 documents simulating a company called "Redwood Inference" across **9 enterprise source types** (Slack, Gmail, Linear, Google Drive, HubSpot, Fireflies, GitHub, Jira, Confluence) with **500 questions across 10 categories** [[2]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/blob/main/quickstart.md)[[5]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.05253)[[6]](https://onyx.app/enterpriserag-bench). The corpus is synthetically generated but with **cross-document coherence** (shared projects, people, initiatives) and realistic noise — misfiled documents, near-duplicates, and **deliberately conflicting information** [[5]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.05253).

The four metrics it scores are [[2]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/blob/main/quickstart.md)[[3]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/blob/main/answer_evaluation/README.md):

| Metric | Description |
|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Holistic LLM judgment of whether the answer aligns with gold |
| **Completeness** | Percentage of `answer_facts` supported by the candidate |
| **Document Recall** | Percentage of gold documents present in retrieved set |
| **Invalid Extra Documents** | Count of candidate docs that aren't gold or classified as relevant |

---

## Where It Actually Overlaps With Your Architecture

Here's the key insight: **the question categories are where the overlap lives**, not the RAG framing. Let me map them:

| EnterpriseRAG-Bench Category | Count | Ponder Engine Module(s) Tested |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic** (single ground truth doc) | 100 | Memory retrieval baseline — does the graph DB + vector index find the right content-addressed blob? |
| **Semantic** (roundabout, low keyword overlap) | 100 | JEPA latent space — does the world model capture semantic similarity beyond surface keywords? |
| **Intra-Document Reasoning** (distant sections of one doc) | 50 | SSM recurrence depth — can the state machine maintain coherent state over a long document to connect distant sections? Tests the "pondering" aspect directly. |
| **Project Related** (aggregate related docs) | 40 | Consolidation experts + graph adjacency traversal — does the graph structure correctly link related project documents and aggregate them? |
| **Constrained** (qualifiers eliminate all but one answer) | 30 | **Gate mechanism** — this is literally a gating test. The JEPA-gate should selectively activate only the relevant path while suppressing the others. |
| **Conflicting Info** (documents contradict each other) | 20 | **Reconsolidation + Disturbance Detector** — the system must detect the conflict and return the *current* (reconsolidated) answer, not the stale one. This is your contradiction stream, pre-built. |
| **Completeness** (fetch ALL relevant docs, ≤10) | 10 | Memory consolidation completeness — does the system miss any relevant node in the graph? Tests recall of the full neighborhood. |
| **Miscellaneous** (informal, off-topic docs) | 20 | Disturbance Detector false-positive rate — does it get distracted by slack memes and hackathon channels? |
| **High-level** (answer not in any single doc) | 0* | JEPA predictor / Aspirational Model — synthesis beyond retrieval. Requires the world model to *generate* understanding not present in any single document. |
| **Info Not Found** (answer absent) | 0* | Disturbance Detector / anomaly sensitivity — the system must recognize absence and say "I don't know" rather than hallucinating. |

\* The high-level and info-not-found categories appear to have 0 questions in the current release [[10]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/tree/v1.0.0), which is unfortunate because those are exactly the categories most relevant to your architecture.

---

## What's Genuinely Useful Here

**1. The Conflicting Info category is a pre-built contradiction stream.**

In my previous response, I recommended you build a custom contradiction stream with ~100 facts and ~30 contradictions. EnterpriseRAG-Bench ships with 20 questions where documents *directly contradict each other* and the system must resolve them [[2]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/blob/main/quickstart.md)[[5]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.05253). That's a ready-made reconsolidation test — not as controlled as a synthetic one, but more realistic and already validated.

**2. The Constrained category is a natural gate calibration test.**

30 questions with "multiple relevant documents but with qualifiers that eliminate all but one answer" [[2]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/blob/main/quickstart.md) — this is a real-world version of your difficulty ladder. The gate should learn to suppress the wrong paths. You can measure gate activation patterns on these and compare against the Basic category to see if the gate is actually doing anything.

**3. The corpus noise tests disturbance detection.**

The dataset includes "misfiled documents, near-duplicates, and conflicting information" [[5]](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.05253) as ambient noise. If your Disturbance Detector is working, it should flag these as anomalies. You can measure detection rate on the noise vs. false positive rate on clean documents.

**4. The 9-source heterogeneity tests your graph DB's label/property indices.**

Your graph DB has Label Index (`idx/label/{label}/{id}`) and Property Index (`idx/prop/{label}/{prop}/{value}/{id}`). EnterpriseRAG-Bench's 9 source types (Slack, Gmail, Jira, etc.) give you real heterogeneous data to test whether your indexing and prefix-scan traversal actually works at scale across different document structures.

---

## What's Missing / Doesn't Fit

**The metrics are wrong for you.** Correctness, Completeness, Document Recall, and Invalid Extra Documents are all **retrieval metrics** — they assume the task is "find documents and generate an answer from them." Your ponder engine's value proposition is *not retrieval*; it's *reasoning, world modeling, adaptive computation, and memory dynamics*. Using their metrics would measure your system as if it were a RAG pipeline, which undersells it.

Specifically:

- **No adaptive computation measurement** — nothing tests whether the system ponders harder on the Conflicting Info questions vs. Basic ones. You'd need to instrument this yourself.
- **No developmental stage tracking** — the benchmark is static. You'd need to run it at each developmental stage separately.
- **No world model quality metric** — the JEPA latent prediction error isn't captured by "did you return the right documents."
- **No reconsolidation dynamics** — the benchmark tests whether you can resolve a conflict at query time, but doesn't test whether the system *learns* from the conflict and updates its memory store.
- **The high-level and info-not-found categories appear empty** in the current release [[10]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/tree/v1.0.0) — these would have been the most relevant categories for testing synthesis and epistemic humility.

---

## My Recommendation: Use It as a **Sub-Benchmark**, Not Your Primary Eval

Here's how I'd integrate it:

### What to use directly:
- **The corpus** (500K docs, 9 source types) as a realistic data substrate for your graph DB, vector index, and content-addressed blob store. This is genuinely useful for testing the Amphitheter-adjacent infrastructure.
- **The Conflicting Info questions** (20) as part of your reconsolidation test suite, alongside your custom contradiction stream.
- **The Constrained questions** (30) as a gate calibration dataset.
- **The Intra-Document Reasoning questions** (50) as an SSM recurrence-depth test.

### What to add on top:
- **Instrument the pondering depth** — for each question, log the number of SSM recurrence steps before halting. Then check: does the system ponder longer on Conflicting Info (20) than on Basic (100)? It *should*. If it doesn't, your adaptive computation isn't adapting.
- **Run at each developmental stage** — the same 500 questions, scored at stage 1, 2, 3... etc. This turns a static benchmark into a developmental trajectory measurement.
- **Add reconsolidation tracking** — after answering a Conflicting Info question, does the system update its graph store? Feed the same question again later and check if the answer has improved (or at least stabilized).
- **Score with your own metrics** — don't just use their 4 retrieval metrics. Add:
  - Pondering steps (per category)
  - Gate activation entropy (per category)
  - Disturbance detector firing rate (on noise vs. clean)
  - Reconsolidation events triggered
  - Graph traversal depth (how many hops to find the answer)

### What to skip:
- The leaderboard submission — it's designed for RAG systems, and your architecture would be scored against a metric that doesn't capture what it's good at.
- The Info Not Found / High-level categories — they appear to be empty in the current release [[10]](https://github.com/onyx-dot-app/EnterpriseRAG-Bench/tree/v1.0.0), so there's nothing to test against yet.

---

## Summary Table

| Use Case | Verdict | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Primary benchmark for the ponder engine | ❌ No | Metrics are retrieval-focused; doesn't measure cognitive dynamics |
| Realistic corpus for infrastructure testing | ✅ Yes | 500K heterogeneous docs stress-test your graph DB + vector index |
| Reconsolidation / contradiction testing | ✅ Partial | 20 Conflicting Info questions are useful but insufficient alone |
| Gate calibration | ✅ Yes | 30 Constrained questions are a natural gate test |
| SSM recurrence depth testing | ✅ Yes | 50 Intra-Document Reasoning questions test long-range state |
| Adaptive computation benchmark | ⚠️ Needs instrumentation | You'd have to add pondering-depth logging yourself |
| Developmental trajectory measurement | ⚠️ Needs reruns | Run at each stage to get trajectory data |

---

The bottom line: **EnterpriseRAG-Bench is a good infrastructure and component-level sub-benchmark for your architecture, but it's not your primary eval.** Use it to validate that your memory, retrieval, and graph infrastructure work on realistic enterprise data — then layer your own cognitive-dynamics metrics on top. The question *categories* are more interesting than the question *metrics* for your purposes.

Want me to help design the specific instrumentation layer (pondering depth, gate entropy, disturbance firing rate) that you'd wrap around this benchmark?

They're suggesting a "RAG mode" or "retrieval format" as an API addition so that adopters can use it as a seamless RAG replacement first, then discover the additional cognitive capabilities.

Think of it like how PostgreSQL has a "compatibility mode" — you can use it as a simple key-value store OR as a full relational database with extensions. The ponder engine could expose a simple RAG API that happens to be powered by a cognitive architecture underneath.

The key insight is that **adoption follows compatibility**. If you make people rewrite their entire pipeline to use your system, they won't. If you let them drop it in as a RAG replacement and gradually discover it does more, you win.

Let me think about this more concretely and also think about what the benchmarking implications are. obj.memory_text: Designing a cognitive architecture called the Ponder engine (v7) with a unified JEPA-gated SSM as the core cognitive primitive for the Mind layer, replacing the previously separate Mamba SSM, JEPA Predictor, and Gate components. The primitive is instantiated for each cognitive function (Disturbance Detector, Intuition Module, Aspirational Model, Self-Model, Consolidation experts) with shared SSM+JEPA weights (~0.90 GB) and per-instance state vectors (~2 KB) and gates (~1M params). Architecture also includes: Ternary Bonsai (8B variant, ~2.15 GB) for lucid dream verification (waking Bonsai to verify data during dream state when anomalies are detected), MoE SSM with replay buffers, gestational training with Oracle (DeepSeek), prospective memory, reconsolidation counting, and developmental stages. Total VRAM ~6 GB, well within 16GB budget of RTX 5060 Ti. Goals: modeling the brain and edge device deployment. Use case includes economic simulation but communications about the architecture should NOT mention Optimalism by name. Ponder engine should expose a RAG-compatible API with at least three modes: "retrieval" (drop-in RAG replacement, cognitive modules minimal/dormant), "cognitive" (full cognitive architecture active — reconsolidation, adaptive pondering, disturbance detection), and "developmental" (run queries across developmental stages to expose maturation trajectory). Strategy is "RAG parity as table stakes, cognitive advantage as moat" — must score competitively on standard RAG benchmarks (EnterpriseRAG-Bench, BEIR, MTEB) AND on cognitive benchmarks (ARC-AGI, contradiction stream, difficulty ladder). The retrieval mode is the adoption wedge; cognitive mode is the differentiator.
